 ![][image1]

**CODING CLUB NITM** 

**Explore Development**

If AI is all you have, what are you without it ?

- Coding Club NITM

# **Introduction**

This Challenge is organized into four progressive tracks:

•        Beginner — Web Development Fundamentals: Build your foundation with HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Node.js, and databases.

•        Intermediate — Full-Stack Development: Develop production-grade applications using React, Next.js, PostgreSQL, and authentication systems.

•        Advanced — Production Engineering: Architect scalable systems with TypeScript, WebSockets, microservices, Docker, and Redis.

•        Bonus Topics: Explore cutting-edge concepts like WebRTC, Kafka, GraphQL, and event-driven architecture. 

 

|   BEGINNER  —  Web Development Fundamentals |
| :---- |

 

## **Web Fundamentals**

After completing this section, you will be able to:

•        Structure web pages semantically using HTML5 elements.

•        Style pages using CSS, including Flexbox and Grid for complex layouts.

•        Build fully responsive interfaces that adapt to any screen size.

## **JavaScript**

After completing this section, you will be able to:

•        Write clean, modular JavaScript using modern ES6+ syntax.

•        Manipulate the DOM dynamically and handle user events.

•        Fetch data from external APIs asynchronously using Fetch API and Async/Await.

 

## **Backend Basics** 

After completing this section, you will be able to:

•        Create a Node.js server and handle HTTP requests using Express.js.

•        Design and implement RESTful API endpoints.

•        Manage sensitive configuration using environment variables.

## **Database Basics**

After completing this section, you will be able to:

•        Write SQL queries to create, read, update, and delete records.

•        Design normalized database schemas with proper relationships.

•        Connect a backend API to a SQL database.

 

## **Developer Tools**

After completing this section, you will be able to:

•        Use Git for version control and collaborate on GitHub with branches and pull requests.

•        Debug JavaScript and inspect network requests using browser DevTools.

•        Navigate and configure a professional development environment in VS Code.

## **Beginner Project Challenges**

Apply your foundational skills by building these projects. Focus on making each project functional and presentable.

 

1\.     **Task Manager** – Build a simple application to create, organize, and track daily tasks with local or API-backed storage.

2\.     **Weather Dashboard** – Display real-time weather information for any city using a public weather API.

3\.     **Personal Portfolio Website** – Design and build a responsive personal portfolio to showcase your bio, skills, and projects.

4\.     **Notes App** – Create a note-taking web app that lets users write, edit, and delete notes with persistent storage.

5\.     **Quiz Application** – Build a timed multiple-choice quiz app that calculates and displays a final score.

6\.     **Expense Logger** – Log daily expenses by category and display a running total and simple summary.

7\.     **Movie Search App** – Search for movies using a public API and display details such as ratings, genre, and synopsis.

8\.     **Recipe Finder** – Allow users to search for recipes by ingredient and display step-by-step cooking instructions.

9\.     **Blog with REST API** – Create a simple blog where posts are stored in a database and served through a Node.js REST API.

10\.  **URL Shortener** – Build a service that converts long URLs into short links and tracks the number of times each link is clicked.

 

 

|   INTERMEDIATE  —  Full-Stack Development |
| :---- |

 

## **Frontend — React** 

Building on Beginner skills: React replaces direct DOM manipulation with a declarative, component-based model. Your JavaScript, events, and Fetch API knowledge now power dynamic, reactive interfaces.

 

Practical skills gained:

•        Build modular, reusable UI components with props and state.

•        Manage global application state using hooks like useState, useEffect, and useContext.

•        Implement client-side routing with React Router for multi-page applications.

 

## **Full-Stack Frameworks — Next.js**

Building on Beginner skills: Next.js API Routes replace your standalone Express server from the Beginner track, unifying frontend and backend in a single project.

 

Practical skills gained:

•        Build hybrid applications with both server-rendered and client-rendered pages.

•        Create API endpoints within a Next.js project using API Routes.

•        Optimize performance with Server Components and static generation.

## **Backend — Express Advanced, Auth & Middleware**

Building on Beginner skills: Your Express.js server from the Beginner track now handles secure user login, protected routes, and file processing.

 

Practical skills gained:

•        Implement secure user authentication using JSON Web Tokens (JWT).

•        Protect routes with role-based authorization middleware.

•        Handle file uploads and manage files on the server or cloud storage.

 

## **Databases — PostgreSQL & Prisma ORM** 

Building on Beginner skills: You move from basic SQL queries to a professional ORM with a schema-first approach, migrations, and relational data modeling.

 

Practical skills gained:

•        Design relational database schemas with one-to-many and many-to-many relationships.

•        Use Prisma to run type-safe queries and manage database migrations.

•        Apply indexing to optimize query performance on large datasets.

 

## **API Design**

Practical skills gained:

•        Design RESTful endpoints that follow industry naming and versioning conventions.

•        Write API documentation that enables other developers to use your API without asking questions.

•        Implement consistent error responses with appropriate HTTP status codes.

 

## **Deployment — Vercel, Render & Environment Management**

Practical skills gained:

•        Deploy full-stack applications to production using Vercel and Render.

•        Manage production and development environment variables securely.

•        Use basic Linux commands to navigate servers and debug deployment issues.

 

## **Software Engineering — Clean Code & Architecture**

Practical skills gained:

•        Structure a Next.js or Node.js project following professional conventions.

•        Write reusable utility functions, custom hooks, and shared components.

•        Refactor code to meet clean code standards and reduce duplication.

 

 

 

## **Intermediate Project Challenges**

These projects require a full stack: React or Next.js on the frontend, PostgreSQL \+ Prisma for the database, and JWT-based authentication.

 

1\.     **Expense Tracker** – Track income, expenses, and spending habits through an interactive dashboard with charts and category breakdowns.

2\.     **College Event Management System** – Allow students to discover, register for, and manage campus events with role-based access for organizers and attendees.

3\.     **Job Board Platform** – Post and browse job listings by category, with application tracking and employer dashboards.

4\.     **Study Group Finder** – Match students based on course enrollment and availability to form study groups with scheduling tools.

5\.     **Online Bookstore** – Browse, search, and purchase books with a cart system, order history, and admin inventory management.

6\.     **Project Collaboration Board** – A Kanban-style board where teams can create projects, assign tasks, and track progress in real time.

7\.     **Learning Management System (Mini)** – Allow instructors to publish courses and students to enroll, track progress, and submit assignments.

8\.     **Peer Review Platform** – Submit assignments and receive anonymous written feedback from peers, managed by an instructor.

9\.     **Hostel Room Booking System** – Enable students to book hostel rooms, view availability calendars, and manage reservations.

10\.  **Social Bookmarking App** – Save, tag, and organize links with a public/private toggle and a shareable profile page.

 

 

|   ADVANCED  —  Production Engineering |
| :---- |

 

## **TypeScript — Type Safety at Scale**

TypeScript is JavaScript with a type system. At scale, untyped JavaScript becomes a maintenance nightmare — runtime errors that could have been caught at compile time cost hours of debugging. TypeScript's advanced types and generics allow you to express complex data contracts, making large codebases safe to refactor and easy to understand.

 

Real-world applications: Every major TypeScript-using organization — Microsoft, Airbnb, Slack, Stripe — credits TypeScript with dramatically reducing production bugs and improving developer productivity.

 

Why it matters in production: A single type mismatch in a payment flow or user data pipeline can cause catastrophic failures. TypeScript prevents entire categories of bugs before they reach users.

 

## **Real-Time Systems — WebSockets, Socket.IO & SSE**

REST APIs are stateless — the server responds to a request and forgets the client. Real-time systems maintain persistent connections so the server can push updates to the client instantly. WebSockets power live chat, collaborative editors, live dashboards, and multiplayer games. Server-Sent Events (SSE) offer a lighter alternative for one-directional streams.

 

Real-world applications: WhatsApp Web, Figma's collaborative editor, live sports dashboards, trading platforms, and multiplayer games all rely on real-time protocols.

 

Why it matters in production: Users expect instant feedback. A polling approach wastes server resources and feels laggy. Real-time connections are the professional standard for interactive applications.

 

## **Distributed Systems — gRPC, Microservices & Service Communication**

Monolithic applications work well until they don't. As systems scale, different parts of an application need to be deployed, scaled, and updated independently. Microservices decompose a large application into small, focused services that communicate over a network. gRPC and Protocol Buffers provide a fast, strongly-typed communication layer between services — far more efficient than REST/JSON for internal service calls.

 

Real-world applications: Netflix runs hundreds of microservices. Uber's surge pricing, routing, and payments are separate services. Amazon's checkout flow involves dozens of independent systems.

 

Why it matters in production: A single large server can only scale vertically (bigger machine). Microservices enable horizontal scaling — run more instances of whichever service is under load.

 

## **Infrastructure — Docker, Nginx & Reverse Proxies**

Docker solves the classic 'it works on my machine' problem by packaging your application and all its dependencies into a portable container. Docker Compose orchestrates multiple containers (app \+ database \+ cache) with a single command. Nginx is the world's most widely-used web server and reverse proxy, routing incoming traffic to the right service and enabling SSL termination.

 

Real-world applications: Nearly every cloud-deployed application runs inside containers. Kubernetes (built on Docker) manages containers at massive scale across thousands of nodes.

 

Why it matters in production: Docker ensures consistency between development and production environments, eliminating an entire class of deployment bugs.

 

## **Performance & Scaling — Redis, Caching & Message Queues**

Fast reads from a database hit a wall at scale. Redis is an in-memory data store that serves cached responses in microseconds instead of milliseconds. Rate limiting protects your API from abuse and denial-of-service attacks. Background jobs and message queues (like BullMQ or RabbitMQ) offload expensive operations — image processing, email sending, report generation — so they don't block the main request-response cycle.

 

Real-world applications: Twitter uses Redis to cache timelines. Shopify uses message queues to process millions of orders. GitHub uses background jobs to process webhooks asynchronously.

 

Why it matters in production: A slow API is a broken product. Caching and background processing are the difference between an application that handles 1,000 users and one that handles 1,000,000.

## **Advanced Project Challenges**

These projects require scalable architecture, real-time features, or distributed system design. Treat them as engineering challenges, not just web apps.

 

1\.     **Real-Time Collaborative Code Editor** – Multiple users edit the same file simultaneously with live cursors and instant synchronization.

2\.     **Live Auction Platform** – Host live bidding auctions with real-time price updates, countdown timers, and bid history.

3\.     **Distributed File Processing System** – Process uploaded files asynchronously using background workers and message queues.

4\.     **Real-Time Chat Application with Rooms** – Build a multi-room chat system with typing indicators, read receipts, and user presence detection.

5\.     **Microservices E-Commerce Backend** – Decompose an e-commerce backend into separate services for users, products, orders, and payments.

6\.     **API Rate Limiter & Analytics Dashboard** – Build a rate-limiting gateway with real-time usage charts and per-user quota management.

7\.     **Container Deployment Manager** – A web UI to deploy, monitor, and restart Dockerized applications with live log streaming.

8\.     **Notification Service** – A high-throughput notification system delivering push, email, and in-app alerts via message queues.

9\.     **Real-Time Stock Market Dashboard** – Stream live stock price updates and render dynamic charts with WebSocket-backed data feeds.

10\.  **Session-Based Multiplayer Game** – Build a browser-based multiplayer game with matchmaking, live gameplay sync, and a leaderboard.

 

 

|   BONUS TOPICS  —  For Curious Students |
| :---- |

 

Explore them at your own pace — even a surface-level understanding will make you a more informed engineer.

## **Real-Time Communication — WebRTC**

WebRTC (Web Real-Time Communication) is a browser-native protocol that enables peer-to-peer audio, video, and data transfer without a central media server. Unlike WebSockets — which route through a server — WebRTC creates a direct channel between two browsers, dramatically reducing latency.

 

Key concepts:

•        STUN Servers

•        TURN Servers 

•        Signaling Servers

•        SDP (Session Description Protocol) 

•        ICE Candidates

•        Mesh Architecture

•        SFU Architecture (Selective Forwarding Unit)

 

Used in industry: Google Meet, Zoom (partially), Discord video, and any browser-native video calling tool. WebRTC is considered advanced because it requires understanding networking, NAT traversal, and media encoding — concepts most developers never encounter.

Project: 

Create a 2 peer webrtc connection application exchanging video much like a video conferencing app.

Constraints: 

1. The signalling server to be raw websockets no [socket.io](http://socket.io) or any other websocket library  
2. The webrtc connection to be raw and no library
